nativeRange.habitat: Muddy margins of streams and seasonally flooded wetlands in tropical lowlands; grows submersed or emersed

imageCandidates.notes: Retailer product photo of potted Echinodorus martii showing characteristic broad ruffled/undulating leaf margins. ~500x659 px. Not a free-licence asset; use only for in-house reference, not redistribution.

Wikimedia Commons category for Echinodorus subalatus (the currently accepted name that subsumes E. martii). Reviewer should pick a specific file with a confirmed CC licence; the species is documented in herbarium and field-photo form rather than aquarium-form here, so visual match to the hobby 'Ruffle Sword' is imperfect.

summary: The Ruffle Sword is a large rosette plant from tropical South and Central America, recognized by its broad leaves with conspicuously ruffled or undulating edges. A heavy root feeder best suited to medium or large aquariums, it tends to be undemanding once planted in a deep, nutrient-rich substrate.
careNotes:
The Ruffle Sword (Echinodorus martii) is a large rosette plant native to tropical South and Central America, where it grows along muddy stream margins and seasonally flooded wetlands in countries including Brazil, Bolivia, Paraguay, Venezuela, Guyana, Mexico, and Cuba. In modern botanical classification, E. martii is treated as a synonym of Echinodorus subalatus, and it also circulates in the trade under the name Echinodorus major; in the aquarium hobby, however, it is still most widely sold and recognized under the names Ruffle Sword or Major Sword.

Adult plants grown submerged typically reach 30–50 cm tall, producing broad, lance-to-ovate leaves whose edges are conspicuously ruffled or undulated — the feature that gives the species its common name and sets it apart visually from the smoother-edged Amazon Sword (E. grisebachii). Lighting needs are moderate; the plant tolerates a fairly wide range of intensities but tends to develop fuller, more ornate foliage under brighter light. Accepted water parameters are 21–28 °C, pH 6.3–7.5, and gH 4–15.

As a heavy root feeder, the Ruffle Sword benefits from a deep, nutrient-rich substrate — ideally 7 cm or more — with root tabs added to support its large root system. Iron supplementation through liquid fertilizer is also generally recommended. The broad leaf spread grows large enough to overwhelm small aquariums; midground or background placement in tanks of at least 110 liters and 75 cm in length is advised. Angelfish and discus may use the wide leaves as spawning sites. Newly purchased plants are commonly grown out of water (emersed) before sale and will often shed their initial leaves as the rosette adjusts to fully submerged conditions — this transition is normal and the plant will regrow new submerged foliage.
breedingNotes: The Ruffle Sword propagates most readily by runners — horizontal stems that extend from the parent plant and produce small daughter plantlets at intervals. These can be separated and planted individually once each plantlet has developed its own rosette and a visible set of roots. Crown division of a mature, multi-crowned specimen is also possible. The plant will flower and set seed when grown emersed or in a paludarium (a tank that combines a water section with a land or emergent area), but it rarely produces a fully developed inflorescence when kept fully submerged in a typical aquarium setup.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Retail care page for Echinodorus martii 'Ruffle Sword': Brazilian origin, moderate light minimum, temperature 70–82 °F (21–28 °C), heavy root feeder needing nutrient-rich substrate and root tabs, iron fertilizer recommended, midground-to-background placement for mid/large tanks, undemanding with broad ruffled/undulated leaf margins used as spawning sites by discus and angels.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Echinodorus subalatus
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Alismataceae, order Alismatales; E. martii treated as a synonym of E. subalatus), full native range (Cuba, Mexico, Central America, Guyana, Venezuela, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay), habitat (mud at stream margins), submersed/emersed growth, and the note that the plant 'benefits from additional CO2'.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Echinodorus major
additional.notes: Used as cross-check for adult size (30–50 cm), temperature (22–26 °C), pH (6.3–7.2), hardness (4–12 dGH), runner-based propagation, midground/background placement, easy care level, and the broad-ruffled leaf morphology. 'Echinodorus major' is one of the trade names applied to E. martii.

propagation: runners / side shoots with daughter plantlets
